metadata.title: Why Filling First Doesn't Mean Leaving Last: The 3d/4s Paradox
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = 4s orbital (fills first in neutral Fe, lower energy in neutral atom); CRIMSON = 3d orbital (stays in ion, lower energy in ion); the two curves cross as electrons are removed
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no self-consistent Hartree-Fock derivation, no exchange-energy calculation, no full transition-series survey. Mechanism only: orbital energies depend on occupation; Madelung filling order applies to neutral atoms; ionization removal order follows ion orbital energies, which differ.

beats.narration_text: Electrons fill the 4s orbital before 3d. When you remove electrons from iron, the 4s leaves first. The same energy argument produces opposite orderings. Something is wrong — or something is missing.

card.copy: Why Filling First Doesn't Mean Leaving Last: The 3d/4s Paradox

beats.narration_text: The Madelung rule puts 4s below 3d. So neutral iron fills: argon core, then 3d six, then 4s two. The 4s fills first because it is lower in energy.
Now ionize iron. Remove two electrons. The doubly-charged ion is argon core, 3d six — no 4s at all. Both 4s electrons left. None of the 3d electrons left. The lower-energy orbital was removed first.
The Question: the Madelung rule says 4s is lower in energy, so it fills first. Ionization removes 4s first. If lower energy means harder to remove, why did the lower-energy orbital leave first?

card.copy: 4s fills first (lower energy). 4s leaves first (easier to remove). How can the same orbital be both?

beats.narration_text: Naive reading: orbital energies are fixed labels. 4s is always lower than 3d. Lower energy means fills first, stays longest. The Madelung rule applies at every stage. So 3d should leave first on ionization.

The mechanism: orbital energies are not fixed. Each electron moves in the average field of all the others. When you change the number of electrons, you change the effective potential every electron feels. The orbital energies shift.

In neutral iron with eight outer electrons, the self-consistent calculation puts 4s below 3d. The Madelung rule reflects this — it is a rule for neutral ground-state atoms. That is all it was ever meant to describe.

Remove two electrons. The effective potential changes. 3d sinks relative to 4s in the ion. The ordering flips. In Fe-two-plus, 3d is now the lower energy orbital. The Madelung table does not apply here — you are no longer in a neutral atom.

The implication: filling order and removal order are not the same question. Filling uses the neutral atom energies. Removal uses the ion energies — and those are different numbers. Two separate calculations; two separate answers.

A chemistry student writes Fe-two-plus as argon, 3d four, 4s two — because she removed 3d first to leave a lower-energy 3d orbital. She checks NIST. The actual configuration is argon, 3d six. Both 4s electrons left. The Madelung table was for the neutral atom; it does not govern the ion.
beats.example_note: illustrative — directly from the card's example seed

beats.narration_text: Why does the lower-energy orbital leave first? Because orbital energies change with occupation. The Madelung rule applies to the neutral atom. Ionization follows the energies in the ion — which have crossed. Same algebra, different electron count, different answer.

card.copy: Madelung → neutral atom.  Ionization → ion energies.  Orbital energies shift with occupation.  Filling ≠ removal.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Formalism and the Three-Dimensional Atom series.

props.seriesTitle: Quantum Mechanics: Formalism and the Three-Dimensional Atom
